chunk_summary: Severe acute malnutrition (SAM) requires triage to determine the appropriate site of care, with WHO-defined criteria guiding management. Facility-based care is essential for complicated cases, while community-based rehabilitation is suitable for uncomplicated cases.
learning_objectives: Understand the WHO criteria for defining severe acute malnutrition (SAM) and moderate acute malnutrition (MAM).
Differentiate between community-based and facility-based care for children with SAM.
Recognize the importance of early SAM detection and appropriate triage for inpatient or outpatient management.
Learn the operational guidelines for managing SAM in Indian public health systems.
key_facts: SAM is defined by WHO as weight-for-length/height below -3 Z, MUAC <115 mm (ages 6–59 months), or bilateral nutritional edema.
MAM is defined as weight-for-length/height between -2 and -3 Z or MUAC 115–125 mm.
Children with SAM and complications (e.g., edema, poor appetite) require facility-based care, while uncomplicated cases can be managed in the community.
Indian guidelines emphasize triage for hypoglycemia, hypothermia, dehydration, shock, severe anemia, infection, and poor appetite.
The inpatient goal for SAM is stabilization, not rapid weight gain, due to risks of overhydration and metabolic derangements.
WHO 2023 guidelines stress continuity of care post-discharge, linking facility care to community follow-up.
reference_quote: WHO standards define SAM using any of: weight-for-length/height below -3 Z, MUAC below 115 mm (ages 6–59 months), or bilateral nutritional edema.
document_key_topics: Definition and criteria for SAM and MAM
Triage and site of care decisions for SAM
Operational guidelines for SAM in India
